text: The California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) fined Pacific Gas & Electric (PG&E) $1.6 billion in penalties for the unsafe operation of its gas transmission system. Most of the amount will be used for improving pipeline safety. The penalty stems from the 2010 pipeline explosion in San Bruno that killed eight people and caused widespread damage. This is the largest penalty ever imposed by the CPUC. PG&E has already spent over $500 million settling claims with the victims and implementing safety measures recommended by regulators. The company pledges to become the safest energy provider in the US. The CPUC's president Michael Picker stated that they failed to prevent the tragedy and must do everything possible to avoid similar incidents in the future.

The California Public Utilities Commission ordered Pacific Gas & Electric Co. to pay a record $1.6 billion penalty for unsafe operation of its gas transmission system, including the 2010 pipeline rupture in San Bruno that killed eight people. The majority of the penalty will fund pipeline safety improvements, with $850 million going towards upgrades and $50 million for other safety measures. PG&E's CEO apologized for the tragedy and promised to become the safest energy company in the US.

The California Public Utilities Commission has ordered Pacific Gas & Electric Co. to pay a record $1.6 billion penalty for unsafe operation of its gas transmission system, including the pipeline rupture that killed eight people in San Bruno in 2010. The penalty includes $850 million for gas transmission pipeline safety infrastructure improvements and $50 million for other safety measures. The company has also paid over $500 million in claims to victims and their families.

Rapper Nelly was arrested in Tennessee on drug charges after a state trooper pulled over his private bus for not displaying transportation and fuel tax stickers. The officer noticed the smell of marijuana, and a search of the vehicle revealed methamphetamine, marijuana, drug paraphernalia, several handguns, and 100 small Ziploc bags. Nelly and another passenger were taken to jail, but later posted bond and left.

Hip-hop star Nelly, known for his hit song "Country Grammar," has been arrested in Tennessee on drug charges. During a routine traffic stop, state troopers detected the smell of marijuana coming from Nelly's private bus. A search of the vehicle uncovered methamphetamine, marijuana, drug paraphernalia, handguns, and small bags commonly used for drug sales. Nelly, whose real name is Cornell Haynes, was taken to jail but later released on bond. No comment has been made by his representatives.
